title: News Feed App - System Design Interview
tagline: Design a mobile news feed client and backend that supports feed browsing, rich post creation, offline use, and efficient media delivery.
problem: Build a mobile-first news feed app where users browse posts, compose rich posts with media, like and share content, open post details, scroll infinitely, and keep previously loaded content available offline.

functional: Fetch a feed of posts using cursor pagination.
Open post details from the feed.
Create posts with rich text, images, and videos.
Like and share posts from the feed.
Support infinite scrolling and offline access to previously loaded content.
Sync offline actions when the network returns.
nonFunctional: Global scale for hundreds of millions of monthly active users.
outOfScope: Content moderation and ranking model details.
requirements.designPrompt: This is a product/client-heavy system design. Emphasize the read path, local cache, offline outbox, media upload, and cursor-based APIs rather than only drawing backend boxes.

capacity.label: Feed page size
capacity.value: 20 to 50 posts per request depending on payload size.

capacity.label: Offline cache
capacity.value: Bounded by device storage, with LRU eviction of old media.

capacity.label: Media upload
capacity.value: Direct-to-object-storage upload with resumable chunks for large videos.

capacity.label: Interaction consistency
capacity.value: Optimistic UI with eventual server reconciliation.

capacity.label: Core assumptions
capacity.note: 500 million monthly active users globally. Feed reads dominate writes. Media bytes are served through CDN and object storage. Offline actions are queued locally and replayed with idempotency keys.

capacity.label: Design focus
capacity.note: The important estimates are payload size, media bytes, local cache budget, and action retry volume after reconnect storms.

dataModel.name: post_attachment
dataModel.note: Image or video attached to a post.

dataModel.name: interaction
dataModel.note: Like or share action with idempotency.

dataModel.name: local_cache_entry
dataModel.note: Client-local cached post or media metadata.

steps.title: 1. Scope the Mobile Feed Contract
steps.description: Begin with REST APIs over JSON because the core interactions are request/response: fetch feed, fetch post, create post, and record interactions.
steps.decisionPrompt: What should the mobile client be able to do online, and which actions must keep working offline?

concepts.definition: Resource-oriented HTTP endpoints with JSON payloads.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is easy for mobile, web, and future clients to share.
concepts.group: Scope the Mobile Feed Contract

concepts.definition: The client updates visible state before server confirmation.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps interactions responsive under mobile latency.
concepts.group: Scope the Mobile Feed Contract

concepts.definition: A client-generated unique key for retryable operations.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents duplicate posts or likes after retries.
concepts.group: Scope the Mobile Feed Contract

deepDives: Call out that realtime updates and push are out of scope, so polling/refetch is enough for freshness.
bottlenecks: Large payloads can make feed scroll janky.
Duplicate offline retries if idempotency is missing.
talkingPoints: Make offline behavior explicit before drawing backend storage.

steps.title: 2. Fetch the Feed With Cursor Pagination
steps.description: Cursor pagination is better than offset pagination for a changing feed because it keeps a stable continuation point even as new posts arrive.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should feed pagination use offsets or opaque cursors?

concepts.definition: The server returns an opaque token that marks where to continue.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents skipped or duplicated results when the feed changes.
concepts.group: Fetch the Feed With Cursor Pagination

concepts.definition: The backend service that assembles posts for a viewer.
concepts.whyItMatters: It hides ranking and visibility logic from the client.
concepts.group: Fetch the Feed With Cursor Pagination

concepts.definition: Returning exactly the fields and media variants needed for a surface.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces network use and improves scroll performance.
concepts.group: Fetch the Feed With Cursor Pagination

flows.name: Feed page fetch
flows.note: The client stores both the returned items and the next cursor.

deepDives: The cursor should be opaque so the backend can change ranking or pagination internals later.
bottlenecks: Hydrating too many posts individually creates fanout latency.
talkingPoints: Mention placeholders and skeleton rows only as UI tactics; the system contract is the cursor.

options.name: Opaque cursor pagination (default)
options.description: The feed is paged with an opaque cursor encoding the position and sort key, so each fetch continues from a stable point.
pros: A stable continuation point keeps paging correct even as new posts arrive at the top of the feed.
No duplicated or skipped items when the feed shifts between page fetches.
Opaque cursor hides server internals, so the ranking/storage can change without breaking clients.
cons: Can't jump to an arbitrary page N — only forward/backward from a cursor.
The server must encode and validate cursor state (position, sort key, maybe a snapshot).
Harder to show a total count or 'page 5 of 20' style UI.

options.name: Offset / page-number pagination
options.description: The feed is paged with LIMIT/OFFSET, so the client requests page N of fixed size M directly.
pros: Dead simple: LIMIT/OFFSET maps directly to 'page N, size M'.
Easy to support jump-to-page and total-count UIs.
Familiar to implement and debug.
cons: A changing feed shifts rows, so new posts arriving cause duplicates and skips across pages.
Deep offsets get progressively slower as the store scans and discards rows.
Mismatch with an infinite-scroll mobile feed whose head is constantly updated.

steps.title: 3. Create Posts With Direct Media Upload
steps.description: Large images and videos should go directly from the client to object storage using upload URLs. The post record references completed media assets.
steps.decisionPrompt: Should media bytes pass through application servers or upload directly to object storage?

concepts.definition: A temporary URL that lets a client upload directly to object storage.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps large media bytes off application servers.
concepts.group: Create Posts With Direct Media Upload

concepts.definition: Chunked upload that can continue after network interruption.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is essential for mobile video uploads.
concepts.group: Create Posts With Direct Media Upload

concepts.definition: A server-side step that marks uploaded media ready for use.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents posts from referencing missing or partial files.
concepts.group: Create Posts With Direct Media Upload

flows.name: Post with media
flows.note: The post is created after media upload has an asset ID.

deepDives: Rich text is metadata; media bytes need a separate path. This distinction keeps app servers scalable.
bottlenecks: Video processing can delay final availability.
Mobile uploads fail often and need resume tokens.

options.name: Direct-to-storage upload via signed URL (default)
options.description: The app mints a signed URL, the client uploads media bytes straight to object storage, then creates a post that references the asset.
pros: Large image/video bytes go client -> object storage directly, never through app servers.
App servers stay lightweight: they mint upload URLs and store asset references, not bytes.
Scales media volume independently; the post record just references completed assets.
cons: Two-phase flow (request URL, upload, then create post) is more client logic.
Must handle orphaned uploads where bytes land but the post is never created.
Signed-URL issuance and expiry/permissions need careful handling.

options.name: Proxy upload through application servers
options.description: The client POSTs the post with media bytes inline, and the app server validates and forwards them to object storage.
pros: Single request: client POSTs the post with media bytes inline.
Server controls validation/transcoding before anything is stored.
No signed-URL or orphan-asset bookkeeping.
cons: Every byte of large videos traverses app servers, consuming bandwidth and memory.
App tier must scale with media volume, not just request rate.
Long uploads tie up server connections and hurt tail latency for everyone.

steps.title: 4. Cache Feed Content Locally for Offline Read
steps.description: The mobile app stores recently loaded posts, lightweight media metadata, and selected thumbnails in a local database so the feed remains readable without network.
steps.decisionPrompt: What data should be cached on device, and how is it invalidated?

concepts.definition: A device-side persistent store such as SQLite.
concepts.whyItMatters: It supports offline reads and fast app resume.
concepts.group: Cache Feed Content Locally for Offline Read

concepts.definition: Removing older or less useful entries when storage is limited.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents the app from consuming unbounded device storage.
concepts.group: Cache Feed Content Locally for Offline Read

concepts.definition: Show cached data immediately while refreshing in the background.
concepts.whyItMatters: It balances speed and freshness.
concepts.group: Cache Feed Content Locally for Offline Read

deepDives: Offline mode is for previously loaded content, not arbitrary historical search. Keep the promise bounded.
bottlenecks: Cache invalidation for deleted or permission-changed posts.
talkingPoints: Mention that cached content may be stale and reconciled when online.

steps.title: 5. Queue Offline Actions in an Outbox
steps.description: Likes, shares, and even post creation can be stored as pending operations with idempotency keys. A sync worker replays them when connectivity returns.
steps.decisionPrompt: How should the app preserve user actions performed while offline?

concepts.definition: Persist operations locally before sending them to the server.
concepts.whyItMatters: It prevents user actions from being lost when the app closes or the network drops.
concepts.group: Queue Offline Actions in an Outbox

concepts.definition: Rules for resolving server rejection or changed state.
concepts.whyItMatters: Offline actions are eventually consistent and can fail later.
concepts.group: Queue Offline Actions in an Outbox

concepts.definition: Increasing delay between retries after failures.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids overwhelming servers after reconnect storms.
concepts.group: Queue Offline Actions in an Outbox

flows.name: Offline like replay
flows.note: The UI can show the liked state immediately, then reconcile when the server responds.

deepDives: Offline sync is where product promises must be precise. A like can be replayed later; a deleted post may reject the action.
bottlenecks: Many clients reconnecting after an outage can spike writes.
Outbox ordering matters for create-then-like workflows.
talkingPoints: Use idempotency keys on every replayed mutation.

options.name: Outbox queue with idempotency keys (default)
options.description: Offline actions persist as pending ops in a local outbox that a sync worker replays in order on reconnect, keyed for idempotency.
pros: Likes, shares, and even post creation persist as pending ops, so nothing is lost while offline.
A sync worker replays the outbox when connectivity returns, in order.
Idempotency keys make replays safe — a retried op never double-applies on the server.
cons: The client owns a durable queue and replay/backoff logic.
Concurrent edits from multiple devices can still conflict on replay and need resolution.
Long offline gaps grow the outbox and widen the divergence to reconcile.

options.name: Re-fetch on reconnect (no local queue)
options.description: There is no local queue; on reconnect the client just re-fetches the latest server state and re-renders.
pros: Simplest client: on reconnect, just pull the latest server state and re-render.
No durable outbox or idempotency bookkeeping to maintain.
Server stays the single source of truth with no replay logic.
cons: Actions taken offline are silently dropped — a like or post made offline never reaches the server.
Directly violates the 'actions must keep working offline' requirement.
Bad UX: the user's offline work vanishes on reconnect.

options.name: CRDT / mergeable local state
options.description: Client and server hold conflict-free replicated types, so offline and concurrent-device edits merge deterministically without explicit resolution.
pros: Local and server state are mergeable types, so offline edits and concurrent device edits converge automatically.
No explicit conflict prompts — merges are deterministic.
Strong fit if many actions are commutative (counters, sets of likes).
cons: Heavyweight: per-action CRDT metadata and merge logic on client and server.
Overkill for mostly-independent actions like a like or a new post.
Server data model must also speak the same mergeable types — a big change.

steps.title: 6. Hydrate Post Details and Interactions
steps.description: The feed carries enough summary data to scroll. Opening a post detail screen can fetch richer text, full media metadata, comments summary, and viewer interaction state.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which data belongs in the feed card, and which data should be fetched only on detail open?

concepts.definition: Fetching compact data for many feed items.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps the feed page small.
concepts.group: Hydrate Post Details and Interactions

concepts.definition: Fetching richer data for one selected item.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids loading expensive data for posts the user never opens.
concepts.group: Hydrate Post Details and Interactions

concepts.definition: Per-user fields like liked, shared, or saved.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets shared post metadata and private user state evolve separately.
concepts.group: Hydrate Post Details and Interactions

deepDives: This step shows performance empathy: not every screen needs every field.
bottlenecks: Detail pages can become slow if they synchronously fetch many side resources.
talkingPoints: Differentiate global counts from viewer-specific state.

steps.title: 7. Serve Media Efficiently Through CDN Variants
steps.description: Images and videos should be transformed into device-appropriate variants and served from a CDN. The feed payload should reference thumbnails and playable renditions rather than originals.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do we keep scrolling smooth for media-heavy feeds across global networks?

concepts.definition: A geographically distributed cache for media bytes.
concepts.whyItMatters: It reduces latency and origin load.
concepts.group: Serve Media Efficiently Through CDN Variants

concepts.definition: A resized image or encoded video rendition for a specific use case.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids sending original large assets to a feed card.
concepts.group: Serve Media Efficiently Through CDN Variants

concepts.definition: Loading smaller assets first and richer assets as needed.
concepts.whyItMatters: It improves perceived performance and data efficiency.
concepts.group: Serve Media Efficiently Through CDN Variants

deepDives: The app should not download full-resolution images for small feed cards.
bottlenecks: Overly aggressive preloading wastes user data.
talkingPoints: Mention lazy loading, placeholder dimensions, and variant URLs as product-performance details.

options.name: Pre-generated variants served via CDN (default)
options.description: The processor transcodes device-appropriate variants once at upload, and the CDN caches and serves those renditions globally.
pros: The processor transcodes device-appropriate variants once; the CDN caches and serves them globally.
Feed payload references thumbnails and playable renditions, not originals, keeping scroll smooth.
Read-heavy media traffic is absorbed at the edge, off the origin.
cons: Pre-generating every variant uses storage and processing for renditions that may never be requested.
Adding a new device profile means re-running transcode over the back catalog.
Slight delay after upload before all variants are ready.

options.name: On-the-fly CDN/edge image resizing
options.description: Only the original is stored; the edge generates and caches the requested size and format on first request.
pros: Store only the original; the edge generates the requested size/format on first request and caches it.
No storage wasted on variants nobody views; new device profiles need no back-catalog reprocess.
Simpler upload path — no upfront transcode fan-out.
cons: First request for a given variant pays a transform latency (cache miss).
Per-request transform cost at the edge can be significant for video.
Less control over exact rendition quality than a dedicated processor.

steps.title: 8. Add Resilience and Observability
steps.description: The final design needs retries, backoff, API timeouts, local error states, server metrics, and client telemetry for scroll performance and sync failures.
steps.decisionPrompt: How will we know the feed is smooth, reliable, and correctly syncing offline actions?

concepts.definition: Measurements emitted by the app such as scroll jank, cache hit rate, and sync failures.
concepts.whyItMatters: Mobile reliability cannot be inferred from server metrics alone.
concepts.group: Add Resilience and Observability

concepts.definition: A mechanism that stops repeated failing calls temporarily.
concepts.whyItMatters: It protects user experience and backend services during incidents.
concepts.group: Add Resilience and Observability

concepts.definition: Server-side or client-side process that corrects derived state.
concepts.whyItMatters: It helps fix count drift from offline and retry behavior.
concepts.group: Add Resilience and Observability

deepDives: For a mobile app, p95 feed fetch latency and dropped-frame metrics matter as much as backend CPU.
bottlenecks: Telemetry volume from hundreds of millions of clients.
talkingPoints: Close with the online and offline user journeys end to end.

functional.how: Cursor pagination returns stable pages, the feed service hydrates compact summaries, and the client stores pages locally for smooth scroll and offline resume.
Media uploads go directly to object storage first; post creation references finalized asset IDs with a clientPostId for retry safety.
Interactions are idempotent mutations that can update the optimistic UI immediately and reconcile server counts later.
Previously loaded posts are served from the local cache, and mutations are stored in an outbox until the sync worker can replay them.
CDN media variants, summary/detail payload separation, telemetry, and retry limits reduce bytes and protect scroll performance.

nonFunctional.how: Stale-while-revalidate reads, local persistence, resumable media uploads, and retry backoff keep the app usable on mobile networks.
Media processing creates variants and CDN URLs so feed cards do not download original assets.
Client telemetry and backend metrics measure feed latency, cache hit rate, outbox failures, and scroll smoothness.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The final design is a mobile-first feed system. Feed pages are fetched with opaque cursors, media bytes move through CDN and object storage, the app keeps a local feed cache for offline reads, mutations are written to an offline outbox with idempotency keys, and a sync worker reconciles optimistic UI state with server truth.
finalDesign.decisionPrompt: Walk through opening the app on a poor network, scrolling a cached feed, liking a post offline, then reconnecting.

concepts.definition: The mobile app owns cache, retry, and offline state.
concepts.whyItMatters: For this prompt, the client architecture is as important as backend storage.

concepts.definition: The server returns opaque pagination state instead of numeric offsets.
concepts.whyItMatters: It avoids skipped or duplicated items in a changing feed.

concepts.definition: Offline mutations are durable local operations replayed later.
concepts.whyItMatters: It turns poor networks into eventual consistency rather than lost actions.

options.title: Full Final Design
options.description: Complete architecture combining every major step from the interview. This is the default wrap-up view.

options.title: BFF aggregation variant
options.description: A backend-for-frontend can reduce mobile round trips and shape payloads, but the offline cache and outbox still remain client responsibilities.

flows.name: Feed read with cache refresh
flows.note: The app shows cached content immediately, then refreshes with cursor pagination and updates local storage.

flows.name: Offline action replay
flows.note: Offline actions are optimistic locally and replayed later with idempotency keys.

deepDives: The final design should be judged by mobile behavior under poor network, not only by backend service decomposition.
bottlenecks: Media payload size can ruin scroll performance.
Outbox replays can spike writes after outages.
Cache invalidation is tricky when permissions or deletes change.
talkingPoints: State that realtime updates are out of scope.
Keep feed summary payloads smaller than detail payloads.
Use direct object upload for media.

followUps: How would you add realtime feed updates later?
How would you cap local storage while preserving offline usefulness?
How do you reconcile a like on a post deleted while offline?
What metrics tell you scrolling is not smooth?

links.groupDescription: Feeds, social graphs, ranking, media fanout, and high-volume social storage references.
links.title: The X Recommendation Algorithm
links.why: Public production-adjacent feed/ranking code for social timeline systems.

links.groupDescription: Feeds, social graphs, ranking, media fanout, and high-volume social storage references.
links.title: TAO: Facebook's Distributed Data Store for the Social Graph
links.why: Production social graph store reference for graph reads, caching, and consistency.

links.groupDescription: Primary papers and docs for storage, replication, partitioning, consistency, and caching.
links.title: Redis Client-Side Caching
links.why: Shows cache invalidation and coherence mechanics for low-latency read paths.

links.groupDescription: Messaging, event streaming, delivery semantics, and async processing references.
links.title: Apache Kafka Design
links.why: Explains Kafka's log, partitions, consumer groups, replication, and operational model.

links.groupDescription: Feeds, social graphs, ranking, media fanout, and high-volume social storage references.
links.title: Finding a Needle in Haystack: Facebook's Photo Storage
links.why: Production photo storage design for billions of small media objects.

links.groupDescription: Core system design references for scalability, reliability, and operating distributed services.
links.title: Monitoring Distributed Systems
links.why: Grounds monitoring and alerting decisions in user-visible reliability signals.
